quiz_questions.question_text: Is f(x) = 2x + 3 a one-to-one function?

options.text: Yes, because it is strictly increasing (always has positive slope)
No, because it has a y-intercept

quiz_questions.explanation: A linear function with nonzero slope is strictly monotonic (always increasing or always decreasing) and therefore one-to-one.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is f(x) = x^2 one-to-one on the domain (-infinity, infinity)?

options.text: Yes, because it passes the vertical line test
No, because it has no inverse
Yes, because it is a polynomial

quiz_questions.explanation: f(-2) = f(2) = 4, so two different inputs give the same output. This violates the one-to-one condition. Restricting to x >= 0 would make it one-to-one.

quiz_questions.question_text: If f(3) = 7 and f is one-to-one, what is f^(-1)(7)?
quiz_questions.explanation: If f(3) = 7 and f is one-to-one, then f^(-1) reverses the mapping: f^(-1)(7) = 3.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the inverse of f(x) = 3x - 6?
quiz_questions.explanation: Swap x and y: x = 3y - 6, then solve: y = (x + 6)/3. So f^(-1)(x) = (x + 6)/3.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does f(x) = e^x pass the horizontal line test?

options.text: Yes, every horizontal line crosses the graph at most once

quiz_questions.explanation: e^x is strictly increasing, so each y-value > 0 corresponds to exactly one x-value. It passes the horizontal line test, confirming it is one-to-one.

quiz_questions.question_text: If f and g are inverses, what is f(g(x))?
quiz_questions.explanation: Inverse functions undo each other: f(g(x)) = x and g(f(x)) = x for all x in the appropriate domains.

quiz_questions.question_text: The graph of f^(-1) is related to the graph of f by:

options.text: Reflection across the line y = x

quiz_questions.explanation: The graph of f^(-1) is the reflection of the graph of f across the line y = x, because (a, b) on f corresponds to (b, a) on f^(-1).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which function is NOT one-to-one on (-infinity, infinity)?
quiz_questions.explanation: cos(x) repeats values (cos(0) = cos(2*pi) = 1), so it is not one-to-one on all reals. The others are strictly monotonic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the inverse of f(x) = x^3?
quiz_questions.explanation: If y = x^3, then x = y^(1/3). So f^(-1)(x) = x^(1/3), the cube root function.

quiz_questions.question_text: If f is one-to-one, what is the domain of f^(-1)?
quiz_questions.explanation: The domain of f^(-1) is the range of f, and the range of f^(-1) is the domain of f. They swap roles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a one-to-one (injective) function?

options.text: A function where each output value corresponds to exactly one input value; if f(a) = f(b) then a = b
A function that passes the vertical line test
A function with domain and range equal to all reals
A function that is always positive

quiz_questions.explanation: A one-to-one function maps distinct inputs to distinct outputs. No two different x-values produce the same y-value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the horizontal line test?

options.text: A graphical test: if every horizontal line intersects the graph at most once, the function is one-to-one
A test to check if a function is defined
A test that checks if the graph is horizontal
The same as the vertical line test

quiz_questions.explanation: The horizontal line test determines if a function is one-to-one: if any horizontal line hits the graph more than once, the function is not one-to-one.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an inverse function?

options.text: The negative of f(x) regardless of prevailing conditions
A function f^(-1) that undoes f: if f(a) = b, then f^(-1)(b) = a
The derivative of f(x), particularly in the context

quiz_questions.explanation: The inverse function reverses the input-output pairs of f. Note: f^(-1)(x) is NOT 1/f(x); the superscript -1 denotes the inverse, not a reciprocal.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for a function to be 'strictly increasing'?

options.text: The function increases at some points
If a < b, then f(a) < f(b); larger inputs always produce larger outputs
The derivative is always positive and greater than 1

quiz_questions.explanation: Strictly increasing means the function preserves order: whenever a < b, then f(a) < f(b). Such functions are always one-to-one.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between the domain of f and the range of f^(-1)?

options.text: The domain of f equals the domain of f^(-1)
They are always the same set
The domain of f equals the range of f^(-1), and vice versa

quiz_questions.explanation: Domain of f = Range of f^(-1), and Range of f = Domain of f^(-1). Inverse functions swap domain and range.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'restricting the domain' accomplish for non-one-to-one functions?

options.text: It limits the domain so the function becomes one-to-one, allowing an inverse to be defined

quiz_questions.explanation: For example, x^2 on [0, infinity) is one-to-one with inverse sqrt(x). By restricting to a monotonic portion, we create an invertible function.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the process for finding an inverse function algebraically?

options.text: Differentiate f(x) in most scenarios involving
Negate f(x), despite what intuition might initially
Write y = f(x), swap x and y, then solve for y to get f^(-1)(x)
Take the reciprocal of f(x) under most circumstances

quiz_questions.explanation: The steps are: (1) replace f(x) with y, (2) interchange x and y, (3) solve for y. The result is y = f^(-1)(x).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does f(f^(-1)(x)) equal?

options.text: x, for all x in the domain of f^(-1)

quiz_questions.explanation: By definition, applying f after f^(-1) returns the original input: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x. Similarly, f^(-1)(f(x)) = x.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the vertical line test, and how does it differ from the horizontal line test?

options.text: The vertical line test checks for one-to-one; the horizontal checks for function
Neither test is related to functions
The vertical line test checks if a relation is a function; the horizontal line test checks if a function is one-to-one

quiz_questions.explanation: Vertical line test: is it a function? Horizontal line test: is it one-to-one? Both use intersections of lines with the graph.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'monotonic' function?

options.text: A function that is either entirely non-decreasing or entirely non-increasing; strictly monotonic functions are always one-to-one
A function with one tone or output under most circumstances involving always
A constant function, as most introductory frameworks would predict when analyzing always dynamics
A function that oscillates, although this interpretation oversimplifies the actual always mechanisms at work

quiz_questions.explanation: A strictly monotonic function is always increasing or always decreasing. This guarantees it is one-to-one and therefore has an inverse.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cryptographer uses f(x) = 5x + 3 (mod 26) to encode messages. To decode, the recipient needs the inverse function. If f(x) = 18, what is the original value x? (Assume the function is one-to-one in context.)
quiz_questions.explanation: Solving 5x + 3 = 18: 5x = 15, x = 3. The inverse function 'decrypts' by reversing the encoding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A temperature conversion from Celsius to Fahrenheit is F(C) = (9/5)C + 32. A weather station receives 86 degrees F and needs the Celsius equivalent. Using the inverse, what is the temperature in Celsius?
A pharmaceutical company models drug dosage as d(w) = 0.5w + 10 mg, where w is patient weight in kg. If a patient requires a 35 mg dose, the pharmacist needs to determine the weight. What is w?
A company's revenue function R(p) = 100p - 2p^2 (for 0 <= p <= 25) is not one-to-one on this interval. A manager asks: on what restricted domain is R one-to-one and increasing?

options.text: R is one-to-one on all reals
[0, 25], where R is increasing since R'(p) = 100 - 4p > 0 for p < 25

quiz_questions.explanation: R'(p) = 100 - 4p = 0 at p = 25. On [0, 25], R is increasing (R' > 0), so R is one-to-one there.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fitness tracker converts steps to calories burned via C(s) = 0.04s + 50 calories. After burning 250 calories, a user wants to know how many steps were taken. Using the inverse, what is the answer?
A chemistry student uses the ideal gas law to express volume as V(T) = nRT/P for fixed n and P. If V = 24.6 liters, what temperature T does this correspond to? (Use nR/P = 0.1 L/K.)

A software engineer uses a hash function f(x) = x^2 on the domain [0, 1000] to map user IDs. A collision occurs when two different inputs give the same output. Is this function one-to-one on [0, 1000]?

options.text: No, because x^2 is never one-to-one
Yes, x^2 is strictly increasing on [0, 1000], so no collisions occur

quiz_questions.explanation: On [0, 1000], x^2 is strictly increasing (f'(x) = 2x > 0 for x > 0), so it is one-to-one. The issue of f(-a) = f(a) does not arise since the domain excludes negatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: A logistics company uses distance-to-time conversion t(d) = d/60 + 0.5 hours for deliveries (d in miles). A dispatcher receives an ETA of 2 hours and needs to find the distance. What is d?
A biologist models a population P(t) = 200*e^(0.03t) organisms after t years. If the population reaches 400, how many years have passed? Use the fact that e^x and ln(x) are inverses.

options.text: t = ln(2)/0.03 which is approximately 23.1 years

quiz_questions.question_text: An audio engineer uses a decibel function dB(I) = 10*log10(I/I_0) where I_0 = 10^(-12) W/m^2. If a sound measures 80 dB, what is the intensity I? (Inverse of the decibel function.)
